titleStmt.title: Napoli, Biblioteca Nazionale, MS XIII.B.29. Edizione diplomatica e interpretativa

msItem.p: Altri testi in prosa e versi

objectDesc.p: Codice pergamenaceo, 146 fogli. Dettagli bibliografici in Guddatt-Figge, G., Catalogue of Manuscripts containing Middle English Romances, Munich, 1976.
Una sola mano, stile secretariale. Mani successive visibili nelle note e nei disegni.

editorialDecl.p: Facsimile ed edizione interpretativa, dove si è provveduto alla normalizzazione grafematica, alla correzione degli errori scribali e all'aggiunta della punteggiatura secondo gli standard moderni.

div.p: Prosa Ricette brevi, 2 o 3 righe, introdotte da un titolo

p.hi: Il ricettario non è attestato in altri testimoni

p.l: o helpe a woman in travel of childe

div.l: Take a da kdark tone and chave it to powdir and ye veyeve it to to traveling woman to
drinke wyne and she shalbe deliuerid anon . a Also take the iuse of leke and yeve
hir to drinke or els yeve hir to drinke detany

p.l: or the disease aftir hir travaile

div.l: Take an oynon and sethe it in watir Ssoft and malwe rote and yolkis of eggis
harde soden and grinde al these to gadir with oyle of olif and make a plastir
ley and ley it hote ther totherto as þe wymmen wol fynde

p.l: o deliuer a woman of childe dede or quike

div.l: Write and put to hur wombe aria peit remedi anna celi abeccam Cecilia
remedi ator arepo tenet oa rotas Also yeve hur betany or sope
her owne watir and sethe ham in wyne and yeue hur to drenke
Take rewe comyn sethe hem bothe in a panne hony and pepir ete a
sponeful at morowe a nothiranothir at none and anothir at nyȝt
Take rede mynte rewe of ei like moche stamp hem wring out the use and
put therof in his nostrel whan he gothe to bed it schal a mendeamende him

div.p:
Trama (in inglese)
Sir Isumbras is a rich and courteous knight, with a beautiful wife and three sons, who has forgotten that he owes his good fortune to God. One day, a bird sent by Christ reminds him of his sin and offers him a choice between suffering in youth or old age. Isumbras repents, and promises to serve Christ while he is young and strong. He calmly accepts his fate as his horse dies, his hawks and hounds flee and his property burns down. He and his family leave the county and wander where Christ leads them, begging for food. As they are crossing a river, Isumbras’ two eldest sons are carried away by a lion and a leopard. Isumbras urges his wife and youngest son to accept God’s will, and the three continue to the Greek sea, where they find the war ships of a Saracen king. Isumbras refuses to convert in return for food and rejects the king’s offer to purchase his wife. She is forcibly taken from him, and he is left, badly beaten, with a cloth full of gold. Before the ships sail the pair is granted a final meeting, where she gives him half of her ring and tells him to search for her. Distraught, Isumbras continues on his way, until a griffin steals his gold and his youngest son is taken by a unicorn. Left alone, he submits himself to God’s will. He joins a community of ironsmiths and spends seven years carrying ore and learning to make armour. When he hears that the Saracens are waging war against a Christian ruler, Isumbras rides a work horse into battle and defeats them, killing their king. The Christian king thanks the mysterious knight and takes him to a nunnery. As soon as his wounds are healed, however, Isumbras dresses as a pilgrim, and continues to wander. He sails to the Holy Land and spends seven years living in poverty until another angel informs him that his sins are forgiven. He then travels to the court of a charitable queen, but weeps instead of eating the feast that is laid before him. Full of pity, the queen gives him a room in her castle, where he regains his strength and beats her best knights in a tournament. One day Isumbras finds a nest containing his cloth and gold, which he conceals under his bed. When the jealous Saracens discover it, they show it to the queen, who realises that it belonged to her husband. Isumbras tells his story and shows her his ring. She produces her half, and the couple are reunited. Isumbras is crowned and decrees the kingdom Christian, but his men desert him when he is attacked by the Saracens. As he and his wife prepare to fight, they are joined by three knights, riding on a leopard, a lion and a unicorn. The five of them defeat the Saracen host, and the knights reveal that they are Isumbras’ sons, sent back to him by God. The family rejoices, and conquers the lands of another three Saracen kings, crowning Isumbras’ sons and converting their inhabitants. When they die, their souls all go to heaven.

Altri testimoni
Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 175, fols. 98r–107r
Edinburgh, National Library of Scotland, MS Advocates 19.3.1, fols. 48r–56v
Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91 ('Thornton Manuscript'), fols. 109r–114v
London, British Library, MS Cotton Caligula A.ii, fols. 130r–134r
London, Gray's Inn, MS 20, fols. 228r228-v
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 61, fols. 9r–16r
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Douce 261, fols. 1r–7v
Oxford, University College, MS 142, fol. 128r

body.l: e made hevyn erthe
al worlde in daies seuyn
that is ul of myȝt
send s alle his bleyng
las and more olde and yong
and kepe s day and nyȝt
wol you telle o a knyȝt
that was douȝty in ilke a iȝt
in towne and eke in ielde
ther durt no man his dynt a bideabide
with pere ne with childe
an he was cuthe y noweynowe
x to drawe in his plowe
and tedis in his talle
man he was curteye and kynde
euery man was his rende
he was lord o alle
Curteis and kynde he was
his name was clepid ir Iombrase
othe curteis and re
the gretti of his curtey
ne couthe ther no man ditry
ulle good man was he
Eke men he louid in halle
and ȝaue ham obus of riche palle
bothe golde and eke e
curteie he was kyng
his curteie had no endyng
n worlde is none so re
s eire a wie had he
as eny in cuthe miȝt be
as telle it ȝowe
and knowe childryn had he thre
as eire as eny myȝt be
for they were eire y noweynowe
Into his hert a pride was brouȝt
of god isGodis worke he had no rouȝt
his merci or to nomme
So longe he vayned in hiȝe de
that od uolde no lengue a bideabide
but end to him a tewyne
So hit bi illebifille ppon a day
the knyȝt went urthe him to play
his orest or to e
and as he went in a narowe way
a owle he hird him to say
that sate ppon a tre
and aid elcome Iumbras
hou hat for ȝeteforȝete whate thou was
or pride and golde an e
he kynge o heuyn wel gretith the
in ȝouthe o elde thou getit pouerte
nd chee whethir thou haddist le tobe to be
ith careful hert and sikyng seresore
he fille ppon his kneys there
and his hondis p he hilde
orldis welthe wol for sakeforsake
and to ristis merci me take
myne hert to hym ȝilde
n yowthe may ride and go
in lde may nouȝt do so
my bonis wol wax olde
ord ȝif thi wille be
yn youthe send hit me
and welthe in myne elde
wey the fowle takith his fliȝt
and al a lonealone leuys the knyȝt
ful sone he went his way
nd whan he had the fowle for loreforlore
his stede that was so liȝt bi forebifore
undir him gan die
is houndis and his hawkis bothe
wenten to wode to him were lothe
ilke one to dyuers way
hat wondir was þan þei he were wo
n fote him heuith for to go
to payne turnyth alle his play
nd also by the wode he went
a litil knaue was to him sent
that come hym a ȝayneaȝayne
he come to tal him a won tithing
that brende walwas al his thing
and his bestis slayne
ord ther nys nouȝt left on lyue
but ȝowre childryn and ȝoure wyue
i that me bouȝt
he seid ȝif thei may on lyue be
mi wife and my childryn iijthree
alle drede me riȝt nouȝt
nd alle he went silue alone
his hurde men he met ilke one
he seidhate eylith yowe
ey seid fee was from hem revid
ord no thingnothing is yowe bi leuydbileuyd
an ox to youre plowe
hey wept alle and did hem ille
the knyȝt bade ham alle be stille
ne wite yow this wo
od ȝewith s and od take
and at is wille riche e make
and pore men also
deleful siȝt he gan ther se
his wife and his children thre
out of the fyre were fled
as nakid as they as thei were borne
they sittith stille ndur a thorne
brent out of hare bed
e chaungid he no thingnothing of his ble
tille he sawe ham nakid be
that ere were comely clad
he lady bade ham be blithe
your fadre come ful swithe
for no thinge be ye a dradadrad
hey wepte alle and made gret care
the knyȝt bade ham leue har fare
and wepe not so sore
lle that care that we be yn
it is for our wikke syn
orthe we were wel more
and we al come no work wirch
our frend of vus schulle be
ouer londe rede we fare

div.p: Metre breve poema a rima alternata
Breve poema in italiano sull'imperatore Costantino e sua madre, Sant'Elena, la scopritrice della Vera Croce.

lg.l:
con mia matre Elena In in
meſtauoce mesta uoce
tro way troway del mio factore quellacroce quella croce
qual tene tucto el mundo Jnsuo in suo domino
